prompt: If photosynthesis had evolved to release chlorine gas instead of oxygen, how would Earth's biosphere plausibly differ today? Think it through; don't just list bullet points.
response:
On an Earth whose algae spew Cl₂ rather than O₂, life has rewritten the playbook for chemistry, morphology, and even the planet’s climate rhythms.

Early on, photosynthetic microbes would have found themselves sitting inside a brew they themselves were turning into a dilute bleach solution.  Any organism that lacked a defense was soon dissolved.  Evolution therefore co-opted every available electron donor to mop up chlorine before it caused fatal oxidative damage.  On the sea‐floor, iron(II) and dissolved sulfide quickly became limiting; the water column that remained became a greenish ocean of chloride.  The volcanic hydrogen chloride carried to the surface from hydrothermal vents meant Cl⁻, not SO₄²⁻, became the dominant anion, giving the oceans a faintly puckering, metallic taste were a human tongue ever to emerge.

Because chlorine is so electronegative, once it is released as Cl₂ it is impatient to take electrons back again.  A thin, photochemically maintained layer of ClO and ClO₂ (with sunset hues of deep carmine rather than our pale stratosphere blue) absorbs much of the ultraviolet before it ever reaches the surface.  It is also opaque enough in visible light to siphon the fiery purple end of the spectrum from photosynthesis itself.  Fortunately, Nature abhors waste: pigments deep enough in the infra-red evolve—chlorophyll-Z, thicker peaks at 740 and 825 nm—to harvest the heat-rich glow of sunlight.  The forests that soon cover the supercontinent’s margins are not verdant green but a bruise-colored maroon that drinks the last photons before chlorine filters them out.

Land colonization occurs early.  Chlorine is dense; accumulations spill seaward off the continental shelves like invisible rivers of oxidant, leaving the upper centimeters of rock and soil comparatively tame.  Early moss-like “sky planters” root themselves not in damp organic loam but in calcium chloride crusts and nitrate pockets, drawing sodium as a counter-ion instead of potassium.  Because HCl condenses at night, thin films of hydrochloric dew creep inland, etching limestone canyons into dagger-blade edges.  Corals have long retreated from the shallows; instead, branching stony plants weave clathrate lattices from trace strontium and barium salts, forming “ivory reefs” that anchor the first crawling arthropods.

Those animals take oxygen indirectly, but not by the hemoglobin mechanism we know.  Oxygen in this world is negligible; rather, hemerythrin-based carriers latch onto perchlorate anions ferried from the gut flora in special digestive caeca.  Oxidation for every heartbeat therefore involves a controlled explosive decomposition: ClO₄⁻  →  Cl⁻ + 2 O₂.  The ventilated blood of this biosphere tastes not metallic, as iron does, but astringent—an echo
